original_hypothesis.title: LockingMultiRewards notifyRewardAmount is vulnerable to fee-on-transfer reward tokens causing reward insolvency and permanent claim reverts
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: notifyRewardAmount transfers `amount` from operator but uses `amount` (not actual received) to set rewardRate. With fee-on-transfer reward tokens, the contract receives less than `amount` but commits to distributing rewards as if full `amount` was received, eventually running out of reward tokens and reverting transfers in _getRewards for all users.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a well-known but still practical integration bug when token types are not strictly controlled. Because addReward is owner-controlled and generic, a misconfigured reward token can brick reward claiming (also aligning with INV-STATE-003 bricking behavior).
deployment_assumptions: A configured rewardToken charges a transfer fee or otherwise results in received < amount in notifyRewardAmount
Operators call notifyRewardAmount using nominal amount, not measuring received delta
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: LockingMultiRewards.s.sol adds ARB as reward token on Blast; likely not fee-on-transfer. However contract supports arbitrary reward tokens via addReward().

proof_of_signal.pattern: rewardToken.safeTransferFrom(..., amount); rewardRate computed from `amount` without checking actual received

preconditions: Reward token is fee-on-transfer or deflationary
Reward notifications occur at least once for that token
Users accrue enough rewards such that contract balance becomes insufficient

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: uses user-supplied amount rather than balance delta for accounting
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: rewardRate overstated relative to actual token balance, leading to insolvency
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no pre/post balance check; no invariant enforcement against IERC20(balance)

analysis_questions: At what point do transfers in _getRewards start reverting (safeTransfer) due to insufficient balance?
Does insolvency brick all rewards due to single token transfer revert (INV-STATE-003)?
Can owner recover remaining rewards, worsening insolvency?
validation_requirements: Test with a mock fee-on-transfer token as rewardToken
Run notifyRewardAmount then accrue/claim and observe eventual revert
Add invariant tests for reward solvency across epochs

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner (or governance) mistakenly adds a fee-on-transfer reward token. Operator notifies 100 tokens; contract receives 98 but sets rewardRate for 100. Over time, claims exceed available balance and safeTransfer reverts, bricking getRewards for all users and possibly blocking other token rewards too.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: High impact to reward distribution; users unable to claim accrued rewards
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires replacing/adding funds or contract migration; no built-in remediation

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests around deflationary reward token support / balance-delta accounting

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor reward token balances vs committed rewardRate

validation_result.impact_summary: notifyRewardAmount uses nominal `amount` rather than actual received balance to compute rewardRate, causing reward insolvency if a fee-on-transfer token is added as a reward token, eventually bricking _getRewards for all users.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The notifyRewardAmount function in LockingMultiRewards.sol transfers `amount` tokens via safeTransferFrom, then uses the same `amount` value to compute the rewardRate. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the contract receives fewer tokens than `amount`, but the reward accounting assumes the full `amount` is available for distribution. Over time, cumulative reward claims will exceed the actual token balance, causing safeTransfer to revert in _getRewards. Since _getRewards iterates over ALL reward tokens in a single loop, a revert on one token blocks claims for ALL tokens. The contract has no mechanism to remove reward tokens (addReward only appends, no removeReward exists), making this condition permanent once triggered.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly present in notifyRewardAmount. The safeTransferFrom call uses the nominal `amount`, and the subsequent rewardRate computation uses the same `amount` (plus any leftover). No balance-delta check exists. The _getRewards function will revert on safeTransfer when the contract runs out of tokens, and since there's no way to skip individual tokens, all reward claiming is bricked.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: safeTransferFrom transfers `amount` tokens, but with fee-on-transfer tokens the contract receives less. The rewardRate is computed from the full `amount`, not the actual received balance. No pre/post balance check exists.

When the contract's actual token balance is insufficient to cover the committed reward amounts, safeTransfer reverts. Since _getRewards iterates over ALL reward tokens in one loop with no try/catch, a single token's insolvency blocks all reward claims.

No validation that the reward token is not fee-on-transfer. Any ERC20 token can be added, including deflationary tokens. Once added, reward tokens cannot be removed (only push, no pop/remove function).

calling_sequence: 1. Owner calls addReward(feeOnTransferToken) to register a new reward token
3. safeTransferFrom transfers 1000e18 but contract receives e.g. 980e18 (2% fee)
4. rewardRate is set based on 1000e18, not 980e18
5. _rewardPerToken accumulates reward debt based on the inflated rewardRate
6. Users earn() reports rewards based on the 1000e18 rate
8. _getRewards iterates rewardTokens and calls safeTransfer for the fee-on-transfer token
9. When cumulative claims exceed 980e18 actual balance, safeTransfer reverts
10. Revert in the loop blocks ALL reward token claims for that user
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: notifyRewardAmount: `rewardToken.safeTransferFrom(msg.sender, address(this), amount)` transfers tokens but may receive less than `amount`. Then `reward.rewardRate = amount / _remainingRewardTime` uses the nominal amount. In _rewardPerToken: `pendingRewardsPerToken = (timeElapsed * _rewardData[rewardToken].rewardRate * 1e18) / totalSupply_` uses the inflated rewardRate. In _earned: reward amounts are calculated based on inflated rewardPerToken. In _getRewards: `rewardToken.safeTransfer(user, amount)` will eventually fail when balance is insufficient.

deployment_assumptions_checked: A configured rewardToken charges a transfer fee
Operators call notifyRewardAmount using nominal amount, not measuring received delta
config_validation.deployment_evidence: LockingMultiRewards.s.sol shows `staking.addReward(toolkit.getAddress(block.chainid, "arb"))` - ARB (Arbitrum token) is not a fee-on-transfer token. However, the addReward function is owner-controlled and can add any arbitrary ERC20 token in the future. No deployment script restricts future additions to non-fee-on-transfer tokens.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is realistic: (1) Owner adds a fee-on-transfer token as a reward via addReward(). (2) Operator calls notifyRewardAmount with a nominal amount. (3) The contract receives less than the nominal amount due to transfer fees. (4) rewardRate is inflated relative to actual balance. (5) Over one or more reward periods, cumulative earned rewards exceed actual token balance. (6) First user to claim after insolvency threshold triggers a revert in safeTransfer. (7) Since _getRewards loops over ALL reward tokens without try/catch, even claims for non-problematic tokens are blocked. (8) No removeReward function exists, so the condition is permanent without contract migration.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH. If a fee-on-transfer reward token is added: (1) Reward insolvency gradually develops as rewards are distributed at an inflated rate. (2) Eventually safeTransfer reverts for that token in _getRewards. (3) Since all reward tokens are claimed in a single loop, ALL reward claiming is bricked (aligns with INV-STATE-003). (4) No built-in recovery mechanism exists - reward tokens cannot be removed. (5) The recover() function could extract tokens but would worsen insolvency, not fix it. (6) All stakers are affected. This is a code defect regardless of whether the current deployment uses such tokens.
recommended_mitigations: Use balance-delta pattern in notifyRewardAmount: record balanceBefore = IERC20(rewardToken).balanceOf(address(this)), perform transfer, then use (balanceAfter - balanceBefore) instead of `amount` for reward rate calculation.
Add a removeReward function or a mechanism to skip individual reward token claims in _getRewards.
Add try/catch around individual token transfers in _getRewards to prevent one failing token from blocking all claims.
Consider adding token validation in addReward to explicitly block known fee-on-transfer tokens, or document the restriction clearly.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LockingMultiRewards with a standard staking token. 2. Deploy a mock ERC20 with 2% transfer fee. 3. Owner calls addReward(mockFeeToken). 4. Operator approves and calls notifyRewardAmount(mockFeeToken, 1000e18, 0). 5. Verify contract received only 980e18 but rewardRate is based on 1000e18. 6. Have users stake and wait for rewards to accrue. 7. Advance time past epoch boundary. 8. Call getRewards() and observe it reverts when cumulative claims exceed 980e18.
uncertainties: The current deployment only uses ARB which is not fee-on-transfer, so the vulnerability requires a future misconfiguration.
The exact timeline to insolvency depends on the fee percentage, number of notifications, and staker distribution.
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz test with various fee-on-transfer percentages to determine exact insolvency threshold.
Test whether recover() can be used to inject additional tokens to temporarily restore solvency.
Verify that the bricking effect extends to all reward tokens (not just the fee-on-transfer one) due to the loop structure in _getRewards.
